summary: Gruber analyzes Bloomberg's report that Apple is planning a new low-cost laptop to succeed the MacBook Air, working through several possible scenarios for what this product could be. He had long expected Apple to simply drop the 12-inch MacBook's price to $999 and kill the Air, maintaining a clean two-tier lineup. Instead, he considers three scenarios: updating the existing Air with a retina display, creating a bigger 12-inch MacBook, or dropping the MacBook Pro Escape's price. After writing through each scenario's problems, he reverses his long-held position and concludes the most likely outcome is essentially a retina MacBook Air — possibly with a redesigned, smaller footprint featuring thinner bezels and modernized ports and keyboard.
key_points: Gruber's years-long prediction was that the 12-inch MacBook would drop to $999 and replace the Air, maintaining Apple's historically clean two-tier portable lineup
Simply adding a retina display to the current MacBook Air while keeping the old keyboard and ports would be a repudiation of three years of Apple's MacBook design direction
A new 13-inch retina MacBook Air at $999 would create confusing overlap with the 13-inch MacBook Pro without Touch Bar, though the current non-retina display at least differentiates them clearly
The product naming is already nonsensical — the plain 'MacBook' is the ultra-thin model while the 'Air' is the mainstream baseline — but consumers aren't actually confused by this

The most likely outcome is a middle ground: a redesigned Air with retina display, thinner bezels, smaller footprint, and modernized ports and keyboard that embodies rather than repudiates recent MacBook design trends
money_quotes: A new MacBook Air that's very similar to the current one but with a retina display would be, to at least some extent, a repudiation of the last three years of MacBook and MacBook Pro design. That doesn't sound like Apple to me.
This is one of those columns where I started with one idea, but in the course of writing it, drastically changed my mind.
We drive on parkways and park on driveways and no one is confused.

The computer named just-plain 'MacBook' should logically be the one that is the baseline best-selling model for the masses. The one named 'Air' should be the one that is as thin and lightweight as is feasible.
A 13-inch MacBook Air with a retina display and a 13-inch MacBook Pro without the Touch Bar are very similar fundamentally.
When Steve Jobs revealed that first MacBook Air by pulling it from a manila envelope on stage at Macworld Expo, there were gasps.
Rather than repudiating the last three years of Apple's MacBook design trends, this new entry-level MacBook would embody them.
key_insight: Apple's MacBook lineup has drifted into naming incoherence, but a redesigned retina MacBook Air at $999 — embracing modern design conventions rather than fighting them — is the most plausible path forward despite the muddled product hierarchy.
